speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Dov Vinograd, D-O-V V-I-N-O-G-R-A-D. Today's date is March 22nd, 1996. This morning, I'm interviewing Mr. Arie Van Mansum, A-R-I-E V-A-N M-A-N-S-U-M. This interview is taking place in Ottawa, Ontario, Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arie Van Mansum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-R-I-E, capital V-A-N, capital M-A-N-S-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the name you had when you were born? Or were you known by a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's the name when I was born. That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Canada, they use me-- they call me, usually, Harry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-R-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any other time in your life where you were known by some other name than Arie Van Mansum?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the wartime, I used the name Harry Van Desande.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-A-N D-E-S-A-N-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the circumstance of using that particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: To fool the Germans. And to-- and if people don't know my name, maybe, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any special significance to that particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing obvious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the city of Utrecht, spelled U-T-R-E-C-H-T, in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Holland. Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Utrecht till age six. And then my dad was transferred to Maastricht. And the rest of my life, I used to live in Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell Maastricht?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-A-S-T-R-I-C-H-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's also in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's also in Holland, correct, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: The southern part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Southern part, is it a certain province?

speaker: interviewee
text: It been province of Limburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-M-B-U-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 5th, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 76 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Gerrit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-R-R-I-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about your father, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was an laborer and later on-- later on a, a conductor with the Dutch railroad. And he also had an part-time job as a custodian of the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other sorts of affiliations did your father have? Did he belong to any clubs, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was just a man of the church, and that was about it. There was not much organization there in Limburg at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And what did your father do in his spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was-- yes, he was an handyman. He made toys for the kids, and fixed the house, and whatever. What had to be  done, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Neeltje, N-E-E-L-T-J-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me about her? What kind of person was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife, she-- a, a dedicated housewife. She looked well after the family and was also, as my father,   an example for the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, he emigrated after the war to Australia, where he died about 30 years ago. My two sisters, both were-- both were unmarried. They lived in Holland. And both died some couple of years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So all your brothers and sisters are  no longer alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: No longer alive, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Tell me a little bit about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother, he-- during the wartime, he had to go into hiding for the Germans because he wanted to work in, in Germany. But he was arrested and was, for a while, in a prison in Holland. And they let him go because of his heart condition. And after the war, he joined the Navy, got  his training in England. And then he met his, his, his, his present wife, his wife that's still alive. And he married [? her ?] in Holland. And they moved, both, to Australia. My sisters, the oldest one, she was a nurse in Amsterdam in a hospital there. And she lived, for the most of the life, either in, in Amsterdam, or in a city close to Amsterdam named Leiden, L-E-I-D-E-N. My youngest sister always was in Maastricht. And she became an teacher in home economics. And she lived with my parents until after their death.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you please describe the city in which you lived when you were about 10 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maastricht was a very old city. And it had buildings that-- churches built in 1200. It's a very impressive city. It was-- however, of course, because we were an, an Protestant minority in Maastricht, we were not well-liked by the people in Maastricht due for our religion. And so we always had a close-knit, close family, together, and members from the church. But it was-- country it is now-- it was very difficult, at that time, to live there as a non-Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the majority were Catholics?

speaker: interviewee
text: 99.9% was Roman Catholic there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Catholics, OK. Tell me about the neighborhood where you grew up around the age of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- as I told  you, my father was with the Dutch Railroad. And when he was transferred to Maastricht, he bought to-- in a common home in a-- in co-op. That was fine. It was with the Dutch railroad. And it was row houses, small row houses. And the majority for the people who lived there were also working with the railroad company. And in that neighborhood, I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What sort of friends and playmates did you have around the age of 10?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the age of 10, most  of my friends  were people, all of them in Protestant school I went to, or from our church.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please tell me a bit about your schooling at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I first went to an public elementary school. And then I went to an Protestant high school until I graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of-- what sort of subjects did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my favorite subjects were math, algebra, I-- that, that kind of stuff. I'm not-- I was not that keen on-- in language, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What sort of school was it? Was it a very strict school? Or was the-- was the subject matters difficult? Was it a friendly place to be in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a friendly place. It was not-- it was not strict at all. And we could-- we could pick some subjects, yes, ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you have any close, personal friends at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily close. You know, we had many friends. But I-- we went-- people were there with them, you know. Not-- some people you were closer to, but not really close. We had that there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were either at school or through the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father worked for the railroad. Was that the main, or perhaps only source of income for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He-- for financial reasons, he became a custodian of the church. And he, with my mom, he cleaned the church. And he opened the door on a Sunday and opened up the doors when there were meetings. And he maintained the, the garden around the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, do you-- did you find that your father had enough time to spend with, with you and your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yeah. Yeah. He spent lots of time with us. I still remember that he bought a second-hand steam train, train. And he played with that. And he let  us play with it. But we usually we were-- we looked at it, how he did it-- does that himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You watched him.

speaker: interviewee
text: We watched him, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I see, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was scared with the steam that came out, you know. But he enjoyed it, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh, I see. Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my family was religious from upbringing. My, my, my parents, we all were members of the Reformed Church, a small church in Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. To what extent did they practice their religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: They practiced their religion by going twice to church on Sunday and by-- at home, you know, educating us, us in the Bible, Old and New Testament, and reading the Bible, and praying on the table, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how was that education conducted in the home? How were you educated about the Old and New Testament at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we read quite a bit out of the Old and New Testament, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the-- on the table, when we have our sitting together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was this read by your parents or each member of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most, most, most was read by  my parents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember any particular moral lessons that, that you started to learn about, at that age, from these readings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the moral lesson we got from the readings, and also from the teaching of our parents, was to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look after the poor, look after the people who are homeless, and so on. And they were an example to that. And we, we followed that  later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that started very early  in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started very early in  my life, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. A slightly different question. When did you first come to understand what racism was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not before the war because there was practically no racism. And as a matter of fact, we didn't knew-- didn't even know who was a Jew or not. But after the-- 1932, when we had an influx of Jewish immigrants from Germany, then we became aware at what racism was and what hate was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? From your experience, what was hatred and racism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only data we, we got was to listen to radio  and the papers what happens in Germany. And that was an eye-opener for us, what racism really was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was being said on the radio and in the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The persecution of the Jews, the way that they had to flee-- leave the country, and came to, to Holland and other countries, maybe. And, and there was quite an influx, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of your immediate or extended family, were any of those anti-Semitic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all. How did you feel about the Jews with this influx?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- our city hall set up a committee when I was 19 years of age to look after those people. And they asked me if-- our church asked me to be a representative there. And that committee  had regular meetings. And we discussed what-- how to handle the influx and what to do with those people. It was upsetting, of course. But the real death came after Hitler came into Holland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry, I didn't hear. How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was one, one of the members of that committee to look after the people who came from Germany and who were-- looked-- who were looking for places to, to stay, and whatever, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this group formed through, through the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was formed with the city of [NON-ENGLISH]-- the city of Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: The city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- OK. At that time, do you recall any episodes of racism in your community toward minorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I'm aware of, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't recall any of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we hadn't ever had a problem, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you recall, perhaps, any of these incidents of racism directed specifically at Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only way I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] know about it is, of course, that people always talk about-- that's, that's a Jew. And I found that-- I found that quite disturbing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they mean when they said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if, if, if, if, for example, somebody bought an-- a, a shirt in a store, if it was done in the store   of Mrs. Johnson, they said, I went-- he, he, he, he-- they treated me not well. But if it was a Jew, they said, the Jew got me again, selling me a bad shirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is, in my eyes, is a form of racism.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first hear of Adolf Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the press, you know, in, in the press and by radio, in-- well, when I was 13, 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember hearing and reading about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we heard his yelling on the radio all the time, you know, if, if he was [INAUDIBLE] speaking engagement somewhere. And we heard about his stand on, on Jews and other minority groups in, in our papers and also in our radio broadcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember his position being vis-a-vis the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very anti-Semitic. He-- as a matter of fact, you know, and it was all known in Holland that he was planning to kill the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he spoke, did he indicate why they intended to kill all the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We, we only became aware of that when they occupied Holland. And they make propaganda with showing the, the, the movie and things like that. And they had big billboards with all kinds of slogans against the Jews, Nazi [INAUDIBLE] mind, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, of course. We were concerned about happened to the Jews and also what's going to happen to all of us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of fears did you have, in terms of what might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have-- we all had fears that he would enter Holland, you know, and occupy Holland, and that he would do the same thing in Holland as that he did  in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your neighbors equally concerned?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- had not close connections with our neighbors, really. Later on, of course, when the occupation came, they showed to be also Nazi-friendly, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. What was your opinion about Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazism was, according to myself, and according to the teachings in our church, was anti-Christian. And as a matter of fact, in, in-- our church had a declaration announcing it, that anybody who became a member of the Nazi party was-- had to be dismissed and couldn't longer be a  member of the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that actually happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That did actually happen, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? OK. ?] Was it in the small scale, a few people, many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them, they resigned beforehand, because, you know, this-- that's what happens, usually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a rough idea as to how many people were involved in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that many, not that many. But it was a small, little [? bit, ?] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did, did your family feel that there was a Jewish problem before the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Were any members of your family, your extended family, supportive of the Nazi movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: None at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Were any in the Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did members of your, your larger community feel that there was a Jewish problem before the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that, as far as they were concerned, there wasn't a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't-- no, there was  not even a problem, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --problem? OK. What do you remember about your country, Holland, just before it was invaded by the German troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews who lived in Holland were very-- still felt at home. And I still remember that if there was an lottery for the-- for the Jewish cause, that our prime minister would-- on the-- on-- by radio, of course, ask the people to buy this for, for, for that-- for that cause, buy the lottery tickets. I still remember our queen, Queen Wilhelmina at the time, had a special seat in the synagogue in Amsterdam. And for occasions, she attended the synagogues. She really was well [? in there. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So at, at the higher levels, people were concerned about the Jewish situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about just sort of the ordinary citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the majority didn't know whether you were or not, because we went along with each other just like anybody else. And when the Germans, here, asked people to have an star on their-- on, on their jacket, or whatever-- then all of a sudden, hey, is that guy Jewish? I didn't know that. And so, so, so, so [? it happened. ?] There was no problem there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just prior to the invasion, what were your non-Jewish neighbors saying about what was going to happen-- the non-Jewish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. No, we hardly talked to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any Jewish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our neighborhood--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --never did any Jew live there at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did the local newspapers print anything in terms of what was impending?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- after the invasion, of course, they, well, had to print stuff that was handed over to them, by the Nazis. Before, they were-- had no-- everybody was comfortable with the, the Jews in Holland. And we had no problem with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And after the invasion, the, the newspapers were forced to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Forced to, yeah. They took the editors away, and put the Nazi editors instead of that. And they make this an-- a propaganda tool. And as result, we all-- we kind of cancelled the subscription to the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Slightly different question, were you involved in any anti-Nazi activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, OK. Describe the day-- as best as you can remember, describe the day when Hitler's army invaded Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, at that time, an, an a patient in the hospital. And we, more or less, expected he was going to do something like that, even if Holland were neutral. And it was a surprise, of course, on May the 10th, all of a sudden, we was-- woke up in the morning, and there were hundreds of planes in the air. And we had-- and, well, it was a shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the hospital at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital, at that time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason for you being hospitalized?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a kidney ailment, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And where was your family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was living in Maastricht, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were they at the time of the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: All lived in-- at their home-- at their  home in Maastricht, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Do you know what happened to the Jews in the larger surrounding neighborhoods where you lived? I realize that there weren't any close by, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away. Not right away. The-- we were, at first, scared. And then later on, they-- everything went back to normal. And we, more or less, believed that there was nothing going on right away. That came on later, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What's-- what-- what's the most vivid thing that remains with you to this day about that particular time of the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the streets were, of course, filled with German soldiers. Wherever you went, you know, there was soldiers walking around in uniform. And also, some pro-Nazi Dutch people went on the street in their uniform. And they had their own thing, set up their own party, the NSB. And, and they had, had the same greetings as the Germans did, the Nazis did. So that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of those people actually welcomed the invasion, and became--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and aligned themselves with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change for the people in your city after the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much except, of course, that, that we had to use food stamps for food. And there was-- less and less food we got, and bread, and meat, and butter, and that kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So food supplies were rationed--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --very--

speaker: interviewee
text: --rations yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --very carefully.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about curfews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later on, they had a curfew. You were not allowed to go on the street at-- after 8 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: From 8 o'clock on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, until early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the next morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you invasion affect the rates of employment or people's jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was plenty of work. It was-- lots and lots of boys, aged 18 and 19 were taken away and brought to Germany for-- to work for the army. So-- and there were quite a few. Then, of course, the, the empty places had  to filled with unemployed people. So that way there was full employment going  on, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were there any new laws brought about right after the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of new laws came into effect, in a way, yeah, set up by Seyss-Inquart, who was the, the man who governed Holland on behalf of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say his name again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seyss-Inquart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you ask me to do this? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: S-E-I-S-Z and a capital I-N-Q-U-A-R-T. OK. And where did he come from? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, he was-- he was Austrian.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an Austrian, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what sort of new laws did they-- did they put into effect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a law of gatherings. We were not allowed to have big gatherings anymore. The, the curfew, of course, was one of the laws. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a whole bunch of new, new laws you had to, to, to try to-- and we were-- we had to hand in our radios, you know. We're not allowed to listen to the broadcasts from either England or America. So we only listen-- were allowed to listen to the German broadcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they do all-- why did they take away the radios and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were afraid we were going to read-- going to hear the truth of what was really going to happen. So we were only just allowed to listen to German propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so they were controlling the, the influx of, of news.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they did, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did-- how did life change specifically for the Jews after the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not right away, but later on, when we all were required to have an ID card with a picture and a fingerprint on that, then they put on the ID cards, with the Jewish, a black J. So anytime they was  in control, they could, right away, see that man is a Jew. And then, of course, they picked up some Jews. It was something around sabotage or whatever they are. That's the Jews' act. And then they picked up some Jewish men in, in, in Maastricht or in other places, and sent them to camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any specific incidents where the Jewish people were persecuted? Did you witness any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one case, one man was, for a reason, arrested by the Germans. And he was-- they brought him a  transport to Amsterdam. I knew his wife. And she was still at  home. She wasn't-- it was non-Jewish. And she asked me to, to go along with her in the train. And so  she talked to me. And I talked to her. And what we said was meant for him. He was sitting in some-- somewhere in the same area with some soldiers who were transferred into Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did, did the Gestapo or any other enemy ever raid your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of the Gestapo raiding the home of someone else who you knew that was hiding Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one particular friend, by the name Van Assen, V-A-N A-S-S-E-N. He was arrested, and he had the two Jewish persons in his home. And they were arrested as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all went-- sent to a camp, and they never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. At any time, did you contravene any of the new laws that were brought in? Did you break any of those laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: The majority did, yes. For example, we were not allowed to have a radio. I have a clandestine radio. And we listened to the-- to the British broadcast to hear what was going on in the war, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So obviously, that was done secretly.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was done secretly, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had that hidden somewhere in the-- in the floor. And at night, we took it out. And then we listened to the BBC.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally know any informers? People who informed on what was going on secretly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Later on, of course, and then I was aware of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you know if any of the people who were informing later on-- were they given some sort of reward or money or something for having informed? Did they-- did they gain from their having been an informer?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans, yes. When you informed for the Germans, they, they, they got, of course, their salary and benefits.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was some sort--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an advantage?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, in my, my particular case, it was, [? do you know, ?] a couple of-- three guys, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and another guy did, Theo [NON-ENGLISH], who were informers for the German, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to be taking a, a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we can continue in just a few moments.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
